coredata.dc:title: Chiral carbon quantum dots for optical power limiting application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Boying
Zhang, Zhao
Luo, Yuxia
Li, Xinping
Chang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: In this research, the introduction of chirality into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) successfully facilitated the formation of chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). These CCQDs manifested excellent photophysical properties. Moreover, chirality was exploited as a key factor to modulate the optical power limiting (OPL) properties of the CQDs. Chiral activities of precursors l/d-threonine (Thr) and l/d-cysteine (Cys) were used to prepare l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD, respectively. The OPL performance of l/d-CysCQD was better than that of l/d-ThrCQD, because the band gap of l/d-CysCQD, containing N and S elements, was smaller than that of l/d-ThrCQD doped with N atoms. The carbon core formed by combining N and S atoms with sp2 hybridized carbon had a structure similar to D–π–A. This enhanced the electron transport ability, reduced the band gap, and improved the OPL performance. Additionally, the Z-scan test results showed a difference in OPL between the l- and d-types, with d-type CCQDs having higher nonlinear absorption coefficients than l-type CCQDs. To investigate this phenomenon, the band gaps were calculated, and it was determined that the d-type band gap was smaller than that of the l-type. This affected the former’s electron transfer and improved its OPL performance. This result provides new avenues for the practical application of CQD chiral modulation in the field of OPL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon quantum dots
Chiral regulation
Heteroatom doping
Two-photon absorption
Optical power limiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images, (b) the images of the size distribution of l-ThrCQD, (c) HRTEM images the images of the size distribution of l-CysCQD.
(a) XPS full spectrum, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s of l-ThrCQD, (e) XPS full spectrum, (f) C1s, (h) O1s, (i) S2p of l-CysCQD.
(a) FT-IR spectra of l-Thr and l-ThrCQD, (b) l-Cys and l-CysCQD UV–Vis spectra of (c) l-Thr and l-ThrCQD (d) l-Cys and l-CysCQD.
(a) l/d-Thr, (b)l/d-ThrCQD, (c)L/-Cys, (d)l/d-CysCQD CD spectrogram, (e)l/d-ThrCQD, (f)l/d-CysCQD optical band gap.
(a)l-ThrCQD, (b) l-CysCQD of FL spectrum, (c) Thr-CQD, (d)Cys-CQD of EEMs spectrum, The CIE diagrams of (e) l-ThrCQD, (f) l-CysCQD.
(a) (b) solution linear transmittance of 80%, (c) (d) 65%, (e) (f) 50% open-hole Z-scanning and light limiting response test.

TG and DTG curves of (a)l-ThrCQD and (b)l-CysCQD (c)l-ThrCQD and (d)l-CysQD of Error bar chart of infrared thermal imaging temperature, (e) (f) aperture Z-scan curves after laser irradiation of l-thrCQD and l-CysCQD.

head.ce:title: Chiral carbon quantum dots for optical power limiting application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710021 China

National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710021 China

Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, the introduction of chirality into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) successfully facilitated the formation of chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). These CCQDs manifested excellent photophysical properties. Moreover, chirality was exploited as a key factor to modulate the optical power limiting (OPL) properties of the CQDs. Chiral activities of precursors l/d-threonine (Thr) and l/d-cysteine (Cys) were used to prepare l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD, respectively. The OPL performance of l/d-CysCQD was better than that of l/d-ThrCQD, because the band gap of l/d-CysCQD, containing N and S elements, was smaller than that of l/d-ThrCQD doped with N atoms. The carbon core formed by combining N and S atoms with sp2 hybridized carbon had a structure similar to D–π–A. This enhanced the electron transport ability, reduced the band gap, and improved the OPL performance. Additionally, the Z-scan test results showed a difference in OPL between the l- and d-types, with d-type CCQDs having higher nonlinear absorption coefficients than l-type CCQDs. To investigate this phenomenon, the band gaps were calculated, and it was determined that the d-type band gap was smaller than that of the l-type. This affected the former’s electron transfer and improved its OPL performance. This result provides new avenues for the practical application of CQD chiral modulation in the field of OPL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the development of laser technology, these powerful energy sources have made people’s lives easier, but they can also damage human eyes and skin. Furthermore, lasers have the potential to induce irremediable damage to high-precision instrumentation. Consequently, for the purpose of safeguarding the human body and specific photodiodes, it becomes imperative to fabricate optical power-limiting (OPL) materials that feature ultrafast response capabilities along with both physical and chemical stability[1–3]. The advancement of these materials has ascended as a crucial frontier in the optoelectronic domain. Their significance is particularly pronounced in fortifying defenses against high-intensity, ultrafast lasers[4,5].

Two-photon absorption represents a specific manifestation of nonlinear optical (NLO) absorption. This phenomenon pertains to the optical power limiting (OPL) behavior where, upon laser excitation, a medium undergoes an instantaneous response. Specifically, it simultaneously absorbs two photons, transitioning from the ground state to the excited state[6,7]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are unique among optical materials due to their simple green synthesis method, high yields, excellent optical properties, and good solubility levels. The carbon nucleus is composed of sp2/sp3 hybridised carbons, with abundant functional groups on the surface and a large number of delocalised π electrons[8,9]. These advantages indicate that CQDs are well-suited for two-photon absorption applications, exhibiting NLO performances comparable with those of other emerging materials, such as MXenes, perovskites, and antimony-based compounds[10–12]. This makes CQDs highly promising candidates for developing green, efficient, and cost-effective OPL devices[13,14]. To obtain CQDs with strong two-photon absorption performances, internal the electron transfer ability is achieved through factors such as the formation of large sp2 conjugated domains and donor acceptor substituents. The specific control methods mainly include element doping, particle size, and surface functional groups[15–18]. Zhu’s team reported two types of N- and S-doped CQDs with stable luminescence intensities, exhibiting good NLO responses and OPL performances under pulsed lasers at 532 nm[19]. Zhou’s team used density functional theory to simulate the effect of CQDs size on nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) from a theoretical calculation perspective. They found that as the CQDs size increased, β increased, and analyzed that this was due to the decrease in the frontier molecular orbital energy gap[20].

In recent years, chiral materials have emerged in the fields of life sciences and materials science[21]. The most notable attribute of its chiral medium is its capacity to alter the polarisation state of the light beam, thereby influencing the beam's scattering, refraction, reflection, and absorption within the chiral medium[22]. Among them, chiral absorption is mainly manifested in circular dichroism. Under strong light, the response to left and right circular polarization will be enhanced and exhibit nonlinear characteristics with the intensity of light. Research has shown that introducing chiral structures (Chiral activated center) can help obtain more efficient nonlinear materials[23]. Consequently, the incorporation of chirality into CQDs will result in modifications to their optical characteristics. There is significant interest in using chirality to control the optical limiting performance of CQDs.

Consequently, in this work, two types of CCQDs, named l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD, were successfully prepared using two chiral sources, l/d-Thr containing N and l/d-Cys containing N and S, respectively. According to the Z-scan results, for the different chiral sources, l/d-CysCQD doped with N and S had a better OPL performance than l/d-ThrCQD doped only with N due to the reduced band gap and enhanced electron transfer ability within the CQDs. More importantly, chiral interactions were applied to obtain controllable OPL properties. There were differences between CCQDs with l-type and d-type structures in the same chiral source. By calculating the band gaps and fluorescence emission spectra of l and dtypes, it was determined that the band gap of the d-type was smaller than that of the l-type. The decrease in band gap affected electron transfer and reduced the transition energy, which increased the nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) of d-type. These results pointed to new possibilities for the development of CCQDs in OPL devices.

l/d-Cysteine and l/d-Threonine are from Aladdin. Both citric acid and methylene chloride are derived from DAMAO. Dialysis membranes are from Biosharp. Deionized water was used for all the synthesis and other experiments. All reagents and chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

Equal quantities of citric acid (1 g) and L and d-cysteine were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water solution and stirred for 10 min until dissolved. The whole mixed solution was then transferred to a 50 ml Teflon lined autoclave heated to 180 °C and kept for 90 min. After the reaction, the container was left to cool to room temperature. The mixture was run through a 0.22 µm mixed fiber (MCE) filter membrane to remove large particles. Subsequently, the liquid is collected and the rotary evaporator removes the aqueous solution within it to obtain the gel substance.

A volume of 5 mL of dichloromethane was introduced into the bottle, and the oil-soluble impurities were removed by ultrasound for 5 min. The aforementioned process was repeated on three to four occasions. Subsequently, the precipitate was dispersed in water, after which a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 0.5 kDa was employed to store the solution for five days. Subsequently, the solution was collected and freeze-dried for 48 h, resulting in the formation of a yellow-brown powder.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the CCQDs were recorded on a Lanthanum hexaboride transmission electron microscopy instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) of the Chiral CQDs was recorded on an S4800. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CCQDs was taken on a Smart Lab 9 kW radiation at a scanning speed of 4° min−1 in the 2θ range from 20° to 50°. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were conducted on a Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of the sample was carried out with a VECTOR-22. UV–Vis-NIR Spectrophotometers (UV–Vis) were recorded on a Carry 5000 UV–Vis-NIR using DI water as solvent. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were using Chirascan. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra, Excitation-Emission-Matrix Spectra (EEMS), and fluorescence lifetime spectra were recorded on the Edinburgh FS5. The nonlinear absorption, nonlinear refraction, and optical limiting spectra of the chirality CQDs at 532 nm were obtained by a Z-scan test system using an Nd: YAG laser system with a lamp pulse width of 170 μs.

The formation of crystalline cores in CCQDs is achieved through intramolecular and intermolecular oxidation and dehydration processes of precursors and solvents. Subsequently, the carbonization of CCQDs is enhanced, leading to the expansion of crystal nuclei, which are typically smaller than 10 nm in size. CCQDs are typically less than 10 nm in size, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to investigate the morphological features of purified CCQDs[24]. As shown in Fig. 1 (a)–(d), l-ThrCQD has nanoparticles with an average size of 3.74 nm. The average size of l-CysCQD is 4.49 nm. In addition, these images showed that the lattice spacings of the two CCQDs were 0.24 and 0.26 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (020) lattice plane of graphite.

To investigate the degree of graphitisation in the two CCQDs, we analysed their graphitisation (sp2 region) using XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak of l-ThrCQD appeared at 26.39°. The peak of l-CysCQD appeared at 25.25°. Both CCQDs exhibited a broad peak at approximately 26°, which was attributed to the formation of (0 0 2) lattice planes in the graphite structures. The peaks of l-CysCQD doped with S atoms are shifted compared with those of l-ThrCQD, which was attributed to the atomic radii of S atoms doped with l-CysCQD have lower angles[25]. This indicates that the two CCQDs have good graphitisation structure and good crystallinity.

As shown in Fig. S1, EDS spectra were scanned to analyze the elements and contents of CCQDs. The elements contained in l-ThrCQD are C, N, and O, respectively. The atomic content is 68.76 %, 7.41 %, and 23.83 %, respectively. In contrast, l-CysCQD contains carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S), with elemental contents of 66.87 %, 8.75 %, 20.00 %, and 4.38 %, respectively. The results indicate that two types of CCQDs containing N element and N, S element were successfully prepared.

XPS was employed to analyze the chemical valence states of l-ThrCQD and l-CysCQD and their molecular structures. The full spectrum is depicted in Fig. 3 (a), and l-ThrCQD contains elements C, N, and O. The C1s element is divided into three peaks, with the peak at 284.80 eV attributed to the C–C and C=C bond, the peak at 286.39 eV attributed to the C–O and C–N bond, and the peak at 288.74 eV attributed to the C=O bond. Similarly, peak splitting was performed for N1s and the sample exhibited two peaks at 400.03 and 401.26 eV, which were attributed to C–N and N–H bonds. The O1s at 531.96 and 533.32 eV are divided into two peaks corresponding to C=O and C–O bonds, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3(e), l-CysCQD comprised the elements C, N, O, and S. Among these, the C element was divided into five peaks, with the first peak at 284.80 eV being attributed to the C–C bond. The middle peak at 286.32 eV is attributed to C–S, C–N, and C–N covalent bonds, respectively, which indicated that heteroatoms had been successfully doped. The last peak, observed at 288.89 eV, was attributed to the oxidation of C=O. The XPS spectra of N1s, as illustrated in the accompanying Fig. 3(g), reflect the presence of three distinct chemical states, each exhibiting a characteristic binding energy. The XPS spectra for N1s, illustrated in Fig. 3(g), show three distinct chemical states with characteristic binding energies at 400.49 and 401.97 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, and pyrrole N. For the O1s spectrum, the XPS spectrum can be divided into two distinct peaks: The sharp peak at 532.02 eV is attributed to the C=O bond, while the peak time at 533.51 eV is attributed to the C=O and C–O bonds. For the XPS spectrum of S2p, the broad peak between 160–166 eV has two peaks at 162.54 and 164.72 eV (Fig. 3i). These two peaks represent C–S–H and C–S–N, respectively. The results indicate that N and S elements have been successfully incorporated into l-CysCQD[26,27].

Furthermore, the CCQDs were characterized by FT-IR to confirm the presence of surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), in the 3,100–3,500 cm−1 region, hydrogen bonding of O–H and N–H groups forms a broad and strong energy band. The peaks observed in the range of 2850–2950 cm−1 are typical features of the sp3 C–H tensile vibration. Both types of CCQDs exhibit an absorption peak at 1700 cm−1, which arises from the stretching of the C=O amide bond formed during high-temperature hydrothermal synthesis. For l-ThrCQD, this peak is stronger, indicating the presence of more C=O groups on the surface. The peaks at 1580 and 1620 cm−1 were attributed to the N–H bending of amide groups and C=O stretching, respectively. The peak associated with C–N stretching at 1235 cm−1 was more pronounced in all the samples. The peaks observed in the 900–1200 cm−1 region represent C–O, C–N, and C–H bonds. Among them, the peak of the C–H bond in the l-CysCQD sample was larger, with a small peak at 1207 cm−1, which was attributed to the C–S bond. No typical S–H stretching peak was observed at 2550 cm−1. This indicated the absence of free thiol groups that could be involved in the formation of C–N bonds. The FT-IR spectra of d-ThrCQD and d-CysCQD are shown in Fig. S2(a) and Fig. S2(b), respectively.

The properties of the precursors used, and the experimental conditions employed largely determine the optical properties of synthesized CCQDs. Citric acid acts as the carbon source, which is carbonized at higher temperatures, while chiral biomolecules act as the chirality inducer. As shown in Fig. S4(e), from 120 to 180 °C, with the increase of reaction temperature, the π–π* transition in the chiral region gradually decreases, and the n–π* region in the conjugated region gradually increases. This observation demonstrates that the chirality is transferred to the conjugated region. The energy difference between the n-electron and π electron systems decreases, making it easier for transitions to occur[28]. The reaction is initiated at 180 °C for 90 min, during which time condensation occurs to generate fluorescent groups. These groups then underwent further polymerization and carbonization, resulting in the formation of CCQDs. Consequently, a series of linear optical characterizations was conducted by the following protocol.

As shown in Fig. 4(c), the absorption spectrum of the synthesized l-ThrCQD exhibited two distinct peaks at 208 and 330 nm. The peak at 208 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition in the sp2 hybrid aromatic domain, whereas the peak at 330 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the synthesized l-CysCQD had two distinct ultraviolet (UV) absorption peaks at 244 and 343 nm. The former peak corresponded to the π-π* transition, whereas the latter corresponded to the n-π* transition attributed to the carboxyl group and C=N/C=S of the aromatic CQDs core. The UV–Vis spectra of d-ThrCQD and l-CysCQD are shown in Fig. S3(a) and Fig. S3(b), respectively.

The incorporation of chiral amino acid molecules serves two main objectives. Firstly, it allows for the introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Secondly, it helps maintain the chiral activity of the CQDs. However, chiral amino acids are susceptible to deactivation during high-temperature carbonization, making it crucial to regulate the reaction temperature. It is established that the boiling points of threonine (Thr) and cysteine (Cys) are 222.38 and 293.90 °C, respectively. The properties of the precursors used, and the experimental conditions employed largely determine the optical properties of synthesized CCQDs. Citric acid acts as the carbon source, which is carbonisation at higher temperatures, while chiral biomolecules act as the chirality inducer. The reaction is initiated at 180 °C for 90 min, during which time condensation occurs to generate fluorescent groups. To investigate whether the two types of CCQDs retain their chiral activity at 180 °C, the circular dichroism (CD) spectra were analyzed. The CD spectra revealed that the two CCQDs had mirror symmetry. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the CD signal for l-ThrCQD was positive, whereas the d-ThrCQD signal was negative. In the UV absorption spectrum, there was an absorption peak at 200-275 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition, and one at 275-400 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition. l-CysCQD had a positive signal, whereas d-CysCQD had a negative signal. The absorption peaks for l-CysCQD were observed between 250 and 350 nm and between 400 and 500 nm[28,29]. These results demonstrate that both types of CCQDs retained their chiral activities during the formation process.

To assess the impacts of element doping on the band gaps of the CCQDs, the Tauc plot method was employed to analyse and fit the UV spectra, allowing for the determination of the band gap values for several CCQDs (1)[30]. As shown in Table 1 , the band gap values of l- and d-ThrCQD are 3.337 and 3.218 eV, respectively. The band gap values of l and d-CysCQD are 3.204 and 3.142 eV, respectively.
(1)
E g
e V
=
1240
λ g

The overall band gap of N-doped CCQDs was smaller than that of N- and S-doped CCQDs, indicating that S doping led to a decrease in the band gaps of the CCQDs (Table 1). This was due to N and S providing excess electrons, which subsequently reduced the energy gap between the highest and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals upon the binding of atoms[31,32]. These results indicated that the co-doping of N and S was most effective in reducing the band gap. By comparing Table 1, it is found that in the N-doped source l/d-ThrCQD, the band gap of d-ThrCQD is slightly smaller than that of l-ThrCQD (3.218 eV < 3.337 eV). In the N + S doping source, the band gap of d-CysCQD is slightly smaller than that of l-CysCQD (3.142 eV < 3.204 eV). As stated in the literature, isomers have different molecular structures that affect the movement and distribution of electrons within the molecule, and steric hindrance is the main reason for the differences in band gaps[33–36]. To guarantee the reproducibility of the experiment, the chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) were synthesized three times under identical conditions. The band gap values were then determined through testing. The data was subsequently visualized, and an error bar graph was plotted to yield reliable experimental results (Fig. S5). In addition, according to Formula (1), the photon energy of a 532-nm laser is 2.331 eV. The band gap of l-ThrCQD was 3.337 eV, satisfying 2.331 eV < 3.337 eV < 2 × 2.331 eV. Thus, both N-doped and N + S-doped CCQDs met the requirements for two-photon absorption[37].

During the formation of CCQDs, a large amount of sp2-hybridized C forms conjugated π domains with orbitals spaced very close in energy. The radiation emission caused by the internal π band gap transition is called ‘carbon core state’ emission[38]. The l/d-ThrCQD solution containing N appeared yellow under sunlight and fluorescent green under UV light irradiation. The l/d-CysCQD solution containing N and S appeared pale yellow under sunlight and exhibited blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV light. Fluorescence spectroscopy serves as an effective method for studying electron donor-acceptor interactions. To evaluate the excited-state electron transfer interactions, the fluorescence emission spectra of l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD are presented in Fig. 6 (a) and 6(b), respectively. Under the same conditions of concentration and solvent, and when excited at the same wavelength of 360 nm. In the same N-doped source, the fluorescence intensity of d-ThrCQD is weaker than that of l-ThrCQDs; In N and S doped sources, the fluorescence intensity of d-CysCQD is weaker than that of l-CysCQD. In different chiral sources, at the same excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength of CCQDs containing N and S undergoes a red shift compared to CCQDs containing N. This observation suggests that CCQDs doped with N and S enhance intramolecular charge transfer. In the same chiral source, the fluorescence intensity of d-type is weaker than that of l-type, indicating that d-type chiral induction can improve charge transfer. As shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d) excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 450 nm were investigated. The spectra revealed that the fluorescence intensities of l-ThrCQD and l-CysCQD, respectively, initially increased and then decreased with the increase in excitation wavelength[39]. The chromaticity coordinates obtained from Fig. 6(e) and Fig. 6(f) show that l-ThrCQD is gradually shifted from a blue-green to a yellow region from the excitation wavelength of 280–450 nm, and the chromaticity produces a red shift. l-CysCQD is firstly shifted from a cyan-green to a blue region from the excitation wavelength of 280-450 nm, and it produces a blue shift and then shifts to red region. These results indicate that doping heteroatoms into the graphite structure of CQDs affects their luminescence characteristics. This effect is attributed to the lower electronegativity of sulfur compared to nitrogen, which leads to a more pronounced color change in l-CysCQD than in l-ThrCQD. Furthermore, the observed bright emission is hypothesized to be a fluorescence phenomenon arising from surface defects, a characteristic closely related to the structure of CCQDs.

The fluorescence lifetime graph displays a double exponential decay, and the fitted lifetime curve is obtained through the process of fitting. l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD were 38, 34, 20, and 18 ns, respectively (Fig. S6). Among them, the fluorescence lifetimes of N- and S-element doped l/d-CysCQD were much smaller than those of N-element doped l/d-ThrCQD.

To investigate the NLO properties of two chiral sources and their l and d types, the Z-scan aperture method was used to study the OPL performance. The sample was subjected to testing under the following conditions: excitation wavelength of 532 nm, pulse width of 7 ns, and frequency of 10 Hz. The testing was conducted in a 1 mm colorimetric dish. By adjusting the concentration of the solution, linear transmittance values of 80 %, 65 %, and 50 % were obtained. To determine the nonlinear absorption coefficient β of the nonlinear absorption coefficient, fit the test results nonlinearly using the following formula.

Fig. 7 illustrates that the overall curve displays a trough shape, which is a characteristic of a nonlinear anti-saturation absorption curve. As the sample moves on the displacement table and passes the focus at 0, the normalized transmittance of both CCQDs reaches its lowest point at 0 when the laser energy reaches its maximum. The Z-scan data for the aperture was employed to derive the two-photon absorption coefficients of solutions with varying concentrations, under a constant incident laser intensity. The resulting coefficients were found to exhibit a positive correlation with the concentration of the solution, with values exceeding 0 indicating reverse saturation absorption.

When the linear transmittance is 80 %, the nonlinear transmittance from Fig. 7(a) is 96.54 %, 96.08 %, 95.47 %, and 93.94 %, respectively. In Fig. 7(b), the optical power densities that achieve optical limiting response are 12.89, 12.72, 12.31, and 12.03 mW/cm-2. The nonlinear absorption coefficients β of l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-Cys CQD are 102.44, 105.10, 116.34, and 123.15 cm/mW, respectively. When the linear transmittance is 65 %. According to Fig. 7(c), the transmittance rates at their low points are 67.26 %, 64.54 %, 59.12 %, and 52.76 %, respectively. The optical power densities that achieve optical limiting response are 8.75, 8.50, 8.25, and 8.50 mW/cm-2. The β values are 154.95, 155.97, 160.31, and 178.62 cm/mW. When the linear transmittance is 50 %, from the curve, it can be concluded that at the focal point, the 100 % passing light is restricted to 62.98 %, 60.21 %, 55.60 %, and 42.65 %, respectively. The optical power densities that achieve optical limiting response are 8.91, 8.49, 8.14, and 7.93 mW/cm-2. The nonlinear absorption coefficient values β are 158.78, 164.54, 168.97, and 180.78 cm/mW, respectively. To compare the effect of laser energy on the performance of OPL. Under the same concentration conditions, change the energy of the incident laser and perform Z-scan on two types of CCQDs. It was found that as the laser energy increases, its nonlinear transmittance gradually decreases (Fig. S7). Data analyses shown in Tables S3, S4, and S5 revealed that, at the same laser energy, an increase in the solution concentration led to a gradual decrease in nonlinear transmittance, an increase in the β value, and an enhancement in OPL performance. However, increasing the concentration causes the solution to darken, resulting in a gradual decrease in linear transmittance. Therefore, balancing the linear and nonlinear transmittances of the solution produces a better OPL material. The results indicate that l/d-CysCQD, doped with both N and S elements, exhibits superior OPL performance compared to l/d-ThrCQD, which contains only N. This improvement may be attributed to the introduction of the S element, which reduces the band gap and enhances the ability to collect and capture photons. In addition, we also found differences in CCQDs between l-type and d-type. The relationship between chiral vectors and structural parameters has caused bias. d-type has a larger beta value than l-type, mainly due to the difference in electronic band gap, resulting in different electron transfers[33].

The release laser irradiation necessitates that OPL materials exhibit good thermal stability. The thermal stability of two CCQDs was characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TG), as illustrated in the TG curve (Fig. 8 ). The heat losses of l-ThrCQD and l-CysCQD were divided into three distinct stages. In the first stage, the weight loss of l-ThrCQD was relatively small before 200 °C. When the temperature reached 200 °C, the weight loss reached 89.84 % and the loss was 11.52 %. In the second stage, the weight loss reached 58.84 % and the loss was 31 %. The weight loss in the third stage occurs after 400 °C, and the weight of the l-ThrCQD sample decreases by about 11.76 %. Upon reaching 650 °C, the rate of heat loss remained constant, indicating that l-ThrCQD had a higher decomposition temperature and good thermal stability. In the first stage, the weight loss of l-CysCQD was relatively small before 180 °C. At 180 °C, a weight loss of 85.12 % was observed, with a loss of 14.88 %. In the second stage, the weight loss reached 30.90 % and the loss was 54.22 %. The weight loss in the third stage occurs after 400 °C, the weight reduction of l-CysCQD sample is about 17.35 %, mainly due to the destruction of graphene carbon network[40]. As shown in the TG curve, the two peaks of l-ThrCQD exhibited accelerated weight loss rates at 229 and 386 °C, whereas the two peaks of l-CysCQD were at 202 and 339 °C, suggesting that this was due to the carbonization of independent molecules or surface oxygen-containing functional groups and the decomposition of amino groups. The thermal stability of l-CysCQD was relatively poor compared with l-ThrCQD, and the decrease in thermal stability may be due to the S-doping, which reduced the bond energy of functional groups. Long-term laser radiation can generate heat energy, causing burns to instruments or the human body. To investigate whether the CCQDs we prepared can be applied to instruments or the human body under laser irradiation, a thermal imaging instrument was used to characterize their temperature changes. Fig. S8 and Fig. S9 displays the infrared thermogram after laser irradiation for 2 h, showing temperature changes over time. In order to visualize the data, error bars were used to present it. As shown in Fig. 8(c) and (d), both curves remain within the appropriate range for the human body within 2 h. Among them, the temperature change of l-ThrCQD is relatively small from 0-80 min, only increasing by about 0.5 °C, while the temperature change is more obvious after 100 min, and the overall temperature change of l-ThrCQD is 1.5 °C within 2 h. l-CysCQD shows the smallest variation between 0-30 °C and rapidly increases between 30-40 °C. It tends to stabilize at 40-120 °C, with an overall temperature change of 2 °C. Subsequently, after 2 h of laser irradiation, to verify whether the OPL performance has changed after 2 h of laser irradiation. Therefore, a comparison was made between before and after laser irradiation. We can conclude from Fig. 8(e) and (f) that there is no significant change in the OPL performance of l-ThrCQD and l-CysCQD after irradiation. This proves that the two types of chiral carbon quantum dots can maintain a relatively stable temperature state under laser radiation, which provides the possibility for introducing OPL materials for human wearable devices and instruments.

In summary, l/d-ThrCQD and l/d-CysCQD were synthesised using N and N, S containing chiral sources, respectively. The Z-scan results show that l/d-CysCQD containing N and S elements have larger nonlinear absorption coefficient values. It is due to the co-doping of N and S elements in the sp2 hybrid carbon core in l/d-CysCQD, which forms a structure similar to D–-π–-A. S doping reduces the band gap and enhances electron transfer, thus enhancing the performance of OPL. Furthermore, the results indicate that the nonlinear absorption coefficient β of the d-type is slightly higher than that of the l-type in the same chiral source. Through band gap calculation and fluorescence emission spectroscopy testing, it was found that the band gap of chiral d-type is smaller than that of l-type, resulting in stronger electron transfer in d-type compared to l-type, which enhances the OPL performance of d-type. Its low price and convenient synthesis process make it a highly promising NLO material. In addition, satisfactory photophysical properties can be regulated by chiral sources for CQDs. This provides new ideas and methods for preparing OPL devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171170, 12004232).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125887.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
